content:
# Par Level Shelf Labels

**Tool ID:** T095-03
**Block:** B095 (Par-Level Stock Management)
**Domain:** Operations & Workflow (D05_operations_workflow)
**Level:** L1
**Format:** Checklist
**Role:** All Staff
**Time Required:** 5–10 minutes
**Version:** 1.0

---
## Welcome

Par levels only work if the person restocking the shelf can see them at the exact moment they are putting stock away or pulling it for service. This checklist ensures every storage location has a clear, visible label showing the minimum stock level — so any team member can spot a reorder trigger without asking the manager.

A simple, printable checklist that turns your stockroom into a self-managing system.

**Built for All Staff working in Operations & Workflow.**
Use it. Adapt it. Make it yours.

---

## Purpose

A practical checklist for creating, placing, and maintaining par level labels on every shelf, fridge door, and storage bin where stock is held. Ensures par levels are visible at the point of use, not buried in a spreadsheet.

This tool supports **Par-Level Stock Management** (B095) and is designed to be practical, printable, and immediately useful in a working cafe environment.

---

## How to Use This Tool

**Who:** All Staff (labels created by Manager or Head Barista; checked by all)
**Time Required:** 5–10 minutes to audit; 30–60 minutes for initial label creation
**Frequency:** Audit labels weekly. Replace damaged or outdated labels immediately.

**What You'll Need:**
- Completed Par Level Calculation Worksheet (T095-01)
- Label maker or waterproof marker and laminated card
- Cable ties, adhesive strips, or magnetic clips for attaching labels
- Access to all storage areas (dry store, walk-in fridge, freezer, under-counter)

**Steps:**
1. Print or prepare labels using the format below
2. Walk through every storage area with the checklist
3. Attach a label to each shelf/bin/door where stock is stored
4. Verify every item from T095-01 has a corresponding visible label
5. Train all staff: "If stock is at or below the label number, tell the shift lead"

---

## Checklist

Tick each item as you verify the label is present, legible, and showing the correct par level.

### Label Format Standard

Each label must show:

> **ITEM:** Oat Milk (1L)
> **PAR LEVEL:** 36 litres
> **REORDER WHEN:** 12 litres or fewer
> **SUPPLIER:** [Name]
> **Last updated:** DD/MM/YYYY

### Dry Store Labels

- [ ] Espresso beans (house blend) — par level displayed on bean hopper/shelf
- [ ] Espresso beans (guest/SO) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Decaf beans — par level on shelf
- [ ] Hot chocolate powder — par level on container/shelf
- [ ] Syrups (each flavour labelled individually) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Matcha powder — par level on container
- [ ] Tea (each variety) — par level on shelf/caddy
- [ ] Sugar (white, brown, alternatives) — par level on container
- [ ] Takeaway cups 8oz — par level on storage shelf
- [ ] Takeaway cups 12oz — par level on storage shelf
- [ ] Lids (flat and dome) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Paper bags (small and large) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Napkins — par level on storage area
- [ ] Wooden stirrers / straws — par level on shelf
- [ ] Deli boxes / food containers — par level on shelf

### Fridge Labels (1–5 C)

- [ ] Whole milk — par level on fridge door or shelf
- [ ] Oat milk — par level on fridge door or shelf
- [ ] Soy milk — par level on fridge door or shelf
- [ ] Almond milk — par level on fridge door or shelf
- [ ] Cream — par level on shelf
- [ ] Butter — par level on shelf
- [ ] Eggs — par level on shelf or tray
- [ ] Smoked salmon — par level on shelf
- [ ] Cheese (each variety) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Salad leaves — par level on shelf/bin
- [ ] Avocados — par level on shelf
- [ ] Tomatoes — par level on shelf
- [ ] Other sandwich fillings — par level on each container

### Freezer Labels (-18 C)

- [ ] Frozen pastries (each type) — par level on shelf/box
- [ ] Frozen bread/dough — par level on shelf
- [ ] Ice cream (if applicable) — par level on shelf
- [ ] Frozen fruit (smoothie ingredients) — par level on shelf/bin

### Under-Counter / Service Area Labels

- [ ] Milk (bar fridge) — par level visible to barista
- [ ] Syrups (bar station) — par level or "refill when X bottles remain"
- [ ] Cups at service station — par level for shift restock trigger
- [ ] Lids at service station — par level for shift restock trigger
- [ ] Napkin dispensers — refill trigger displayed

### Cleaning Supplies Storage

- [ ] Sanitiser spray — par level on shelf
- [ ] Blue roll / paper towels — par level on shelf
- [ ] Bin bags — par level on shelf
- [ ] Dishwasher detergent — par level on shelf
- [ ] Milk jug cleaner / Cafiza — par level on shelf

### Label Quality Audit

- [ ] All labels are legible (not faded, smudged, or torn)
- [ ] All labels show the correct, current par level (cross-check with T095-01)
- [ ] All labels are waterproof or laminated (especially fridge/freezer labels)
- [ ] All labels are securely attached (not falling off)
- [ ] Labels are positioned at eye level where the item is stored
- [ ] Date of last update is within the past 30 days on all labels
- [ ] New items added to the menu have corresponding par level labels

### Sign-Off

| Checked by | Date | Labels needing replacement | Notes |
|-----------|------|--------------------------|-------|
| *e.g., Aiste* | *e.g., 15/03/2026* | *e.g., oat milk fridge label faded* | *Replaced same day* |
| | | | |

---

## Common Risks

### Risk 1: Labels Fall Off in Fridge/Freezer

**Why it happens:** Adhesive fails in cold, damp conditions. Paper labels disintegrate within days.
**How to prevent:** Use laminated cards with magnetic clips or cable ties. Never use paper sticky notes in fridges or freezers.
**If it's already happening:** Replace with laminated labels today. Cable-tie them to wire shelving.

### Risk 2: Par Levels Change But Labels Stay the Same

**Why it happens:** Manager updates the spreadsheet but forgets to update physical labels. Staff reorder based on stale numbers.
**How to prevent:** Every time T095-01 is reviewed, the reviewer must walk the storage areas and update labels in the same session. Make it one task, not two.
**If it's already happening:** Schedule a full label audit this week using this checklist.

### Risk 3: Staff Ignore the Labels

**Why it happens:** Labels are present but nobody was told what to do when stock hits the par level.
**How to prevent:** The training is one sentence: "If stock is at or below the number on the label, tell the shift lead immediately." Reinforce in every team briefing for the first month.
**If it's already happening:** Run a 2-minute refresher at the next shift handover. Point at a label, ask "what do you do when it hits this number?"

---

## Customisation Notes

**This tool is a starting point, not a rulebook.**

**Independent / Specialty Cafe:** Add labels for guest coffees, seasonal items, and specialty ingredients. Your stockroom is your competitive advantage — make it visually obvious.
**Chain / Franchise:** Use brand-standard label templates if provided. Supplement with par-level data from T095-01.
**Kiosk / Mobile:** Fewer items, but every item is critical. Labels on every shelf/bin — no exceptions.
**Bakery Cafe:** Add labels for flour bins, yeast, proving agents, and display case minimums.

**Making it yours:**
- Colour-code labels by category (green = dairy, blue = dry goods, red = cleaning)
- Add supplier phone number to the label for urgent reorders
- Include a QR code linking to the digital order form if your team uses tablets
- Laminate a master checklist and pin it inside the stockroom door

The structure matters more than the specific fields. Keep the structure, change the content.

---

## Connected Resources

**Other tools in this block:**
- T095-01 — Par Level Calculation Worksheet (L1, template)
- T095-02 — Weekly Order Template (L2, template)
- T095-04 — Monthly Par Level Review Report (L3, dashboard)

**Related blocks:**

**Parent block:** B095 — Par-Level Stock Management
**Module:** M05: Service Flow & Peak Management
---

*Tool Version 1.0 — OIS Cafe v2*
*Built for cafe operators who value clarity, consistency, and craft.*
